title: Non-overlapping Intervals
description:
You're handed a list `intervals`, each entry shaped like `[start, end]`. Some of them overlap in time. Figure out the fewest intervals you'd need to delete so that whatever remains has zero overlaps. Two intervals that merely touch at a shared endpoint -- one ending exactly where the next begins -- are considered fine, not a conflict.

**Example**

```
intervals = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[1,3]]  ->  1
```
(Removing `[1,3]` leaves `[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]`, which don't overlap.)
hints: This is the classic activity-selection problem in disguise: maximize the number of intervals you KEEP (non-overlapping), then the answer is total count minus that maximum.
Sort intervals by their end value. Greedily keep an interval if its start is at or after the end of the last interval you kept; a smaller end leaves more room for future intervals.
Track how many intervals you kept as you scan; the removals needed is len(intervals) minus that count.

solutions.name: Greedy, sort by end
solutions.explanation: Sorting by end time and greedily keeping an interval whenever it starts at or after the end of the last kept interval maximizes the count of non-overlapping intervals we can retain (classic interval scheduling / activity selection). Every interval we don't keep must be removed, so the answer is the total number of intervals minus the number kept.
solutions.complexity: Time O(n log n), Space O(n)
